drafts.draft: Most small businesses don't have a marketing problem. They have a follow-up problem. The leads are already coming in. Nobody's chasing them on Tuesday at 6pm.
If your customer asks a question at 9pm and you answer at 9am, the sale is gone. Speed has quietly become the only differentiator left.
Founders spend years building the perfect onboarding flow then lose 40% of signups because nobody replies to the first 'is this legit?' email for 6 hours.
drafts.reason: Specific funnel pain, no banned words

drafts.target_text: Honest take on AI for small business in 2026: Most of the hype is still hype. But automated follow-up, review management...
drafts.draft: Agreed. The boring middle layer (follow-up, scheduling, review chasing) is where AI actually pays back. Demos and chat-bots are theater.
drafts.reason: On-topic SMB, agree-and-extend (sub-1k but on-message and fresh)

drafts.target_text: Compounding loss is underrated. 15-yr field tech at a manufacturer walks out = ~8k undocumented decisions gone.
drafts.draft: The frame I keep coming back to: tribal knowledge isn't lost when someone leaves, it's lost the day they stopped writing it down.
drafts.reason: Warm chain, pushes the metaphor forward

drafts.draft: And it always runs out a week before the thing that would've worked starts working.
The companies that figured this out early stopped calling it support and started calling it the onboarding team.
Until the third rewrite. Then it's actually simple.
Building public is easy. Writing about what didn't work is the harder, more useful version.
drafts.reason: Warm chain, on-topic for builder audience

drafts.draft: Building in public is undefeated for one reason: nobody else is willing to show the receipts.
